section.title: The Bride's Invitation
section.sectionRationale: The bridegroom praises his bride once more, now from feet to head, delighting in her whole person, and declares how fair and pleasant she is. The bride answers with the second great refrain of belonging, I belong to my beloved, and his desire is for me, and then invites him away to the countryside and the vineyards, where she will give him her love. Desire here is mutual, glad, and freely offered within covenant.
section.thesis: The bridegroom's delight in his bride is met by her glad, confident answer: she belongs to him and his desire is for her, and she invites him out to the vineyards to give him her love, mutual desire offered freely within covenant.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the bridegroom's praise from feet to head, captivated by his bride; her answering refrain, I belong to my beloved, and his desire is for me; and her springtime invitation to the vineyards, there I will give you my love.

verses.text: How beautiful are your sandaled feet, O daughter of the prince! The curves of your thighs are like jewels, the handiwork of a master.
Your navel is a rounded goblet; it never lacks blended wine. Your waist is a mound of wheat encircled by the lilies.
Your breasts are like two fawns, twins of a gazelle.
Your neck is like a tower made of ivory; your eyes are like the pools of Heshbon by the gate of Bath-rabbim; your nose is like the tower of Lebanon, facing toward Damascus.
Your head crowns you like Mount Carmel, the hair of your head like purple threads; the king is captured in your tresses.
How fair and pleasant you are, O {{ahavah|love}}, with your delights!
Your stature is like a palm tree; your breasts are clusters of fruit.
I said, “I will climb the palm tree; I will take hold of its fruit.” May your breasts be like clusters of the vine, the fragrance of your breath like apples,
and your mouth like the finest wine. May it flow smoothly to my beloved, gliding gently over lips and teeth.
I belong to my {{dodi|beloved}}, and his {{teshuqah|desire}} is for me.

units.label: Come to the vineyards
units.note: there I will give you my love

verses.text: Come, my beloved, let us go to the countryside; let us spend the night among the wildflowers.
Let us go early to the vineyards to see if the vine has budded, if the blossom has opened, if the pomegranates are in bloom, there I will give you my love.
The mandrakes send forth a fragrance, and at our door is every delicacy, new as well as old, that I have treasured up for you, my beloved.

ahavah.gloss: Love. The bridegroom calls the bride herself O love, with your delights; the same word that crowns the Song at 8:6, covenant love here personified in the beloved and delighted in.

dodi.gloss: My beloved. The bride's recurring name for the man; her refrain, I belong to my beloved, and his desire is for me, is the settled language of mutual, covenant belonging.

teshuqah.gloss: Desire, longing. A rare word: it appears of the woman's desire toward her husband in Genesis 3:16 and of sin crouching in Genesis 4:7. Here it is turned to joy, his desire is for me, longing redeemed and freely returned within covenant love.

basic.context: The bridegroom praises his bride once more, this time from her feet upward, delighting in her whole person and crying, how fair and pleasant you are, O love. He longs to draw near to her. The bride answers with the second great refrain of the Song, I belong to my beloved, and his desire is for me, and then she takes the lead, inviting him out to the countryside and the vineyards in the fresh of the morning, there I will give you my love. Desire here is mutual, unhurried, and freely offered.
basic.hermeneutics: On the plain level this is the glad, mutual desire of husband and wife, freely given within their covenant, the wife now inviting as gladly as the husband praises. The imagery is poetic and evocative, not clinical. The word desire is striking: it is the very word used in Genesis 3 for the strained longing between the sexes, here healed and turned to joy. Within the whole Bible, the mutual belonging and the longing satisfied point to the love between Christ and His church.
basic.originalLanguages: The bridegroom calls the bride herself ahavah, love, and says his heart is captured. She answers with dodi, my beloved, and the rare word teshuqah, desire, the very word from Genesis 3:16, now redeemed: his desire is for me. Then she invites him to the vineyards, there I will give you my love, longing freely returned rather than grasped.
basic.typology: The mutual desire of bridegroom and bride, each glad to belong to the other, foreshadows the love between Christ and His church, His delight in her and her longing for Him. The word desire, once strained by the fall, healed here into joy, points to the restoration of love in Christ.
basic.christ: The refrain his desire is for me pictures the astonishing truth that Christ desires His people, that He set His love on the church and longs for her. And the bride's glad invitation pictures the church's answering longing, come, my beloved, the same cry the Spirit and the bride raise at the close of Scripture, come, Lord Jesus.
basic.god: God is not reluctant toward His people; His desire is for them. He heals what the fall strained, turning grasping and rule into mutual delight, and He receives the love His people freely offer. The intimacy of husband and wife is His good gift, and He is not embarrassed by joy.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 3:16, the word desire, strained by the fall and healed here. Ephesians 5:31 to 32, the one flesh mystery of Christ and the church. Revelation 22:17, the Spirit and the bride say, come. Isaiah 62:5, as a bridegroom rejoices over the bride, so will your God rejoice over you.
basic.oneStory: Praise is met with glad answer: the bride belongs to her beloved, his desire is for her, and she freely invites him to the vineyards. Mutual longing, once strained by the fall, is healed into joy, a picture of the love between Christ and His church, who together say, come.

context.literary: A third wasf, now from feet to head, mirroring the head to foot praise of chapter 4, and answered not by silence but by the bride's own refrain and invitation. The movement runs from the bridegroom's praise, to the pivotal refrain of belonging at 7:10, to the bride's springtime invitation, echoing the invitation of chapter 2.
context.historical: The praise draws on a rich landscape: the pools of Heshbon, the tower of Lebanon facing Damascus, Mount Carmel, purple threads of royal dye, palm trees, vineyards, and mandrakes, an ancient symbol of love and fruitfulness. The invitation to go early to the vineyards fits the rhythms of the agricultural year.
context.canonical: The refrain of mutual belonging and the answering cry come, my beloved, thread through Scripture to the marriage of the Lamb, where the Spirit and the bride say, come, and the word desire, first strained in Genesis 3, is set within the redeemed longing of covenant love.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show the bridegroom's delight in his bride met by her glad, confident answer of belonging and her free invitation, presenting mutual desire as good and joyful within covenant.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes the lovers' mutual delight rather than commanding it, yet it carries an abiding vision: within covenant, desire is not shameful but good, mutual, and freely offered, the longing of the fall healed into joy.

debates.question: What kind of book is the Song: a celebration of married love, an allegory of Christ and the church, or both?

views.label: A celebration of covenant married love
views.case: This plainly celebrates the glad, mutual desire of husband and wife, the wife inviting as freely as the husband praises; Scripture honors such delight within marriage, Proverbs 5:18 to 19 and 1 Corinthians 7:3 to 5.

views.label: An allegory of Christ and the church, or God and Israel
views.case: The church has long heard in his desire is for me and come, my beloved the love between Christ and His people, His longing for the church and hers for Him, in keeping with Ephesians 5 and Revelation 22:17.

views.label: Both, the literal love as a God-given sign
views.case: The wisest reading holds both: the poem truly celebrates the mutual desire of husband and wife, and because that union is a God-given sign of Christ and the church, Ephesians 5:31 to 32, the refrain his desire is for me rightly points to the love of Christ for His own.

debates.bearing: Holding both keeps the passage reverent: it honors mutual marital desire as good and joyful, neither shameful nor to be spiritualized away, and follows it to the love between Christ and His church.

study.originalLanguages: The bridegroom's praise climbs from sandaled feet to crowning head and breaks into direct address, ahavah, love, how fair and pleasant you are. The bride answers with the settled refrain built on dodi, my beloved, and the rare word teshuqah, desire. That word is heavy with memory: in Genesis 3:16 it names the strained longing between the sexes after the fall, and in Genesis 4:7 sin's desire to master. Here it is turned toward joy, his desire is for me, longing redeemed and freely returned. Then she leads, come, my beloved, to the vineyards, there I will give you my love.

typology.type: The mutual desire of bridegroom and bride within covenant
typology.fulfillment: The love between Christ and His church, His delight in her and her longing for Him, Ephesians 5:25 to 32.

typology.type: The word desire, strained in Genesis 3, healed here into joy
typology.fulfillment: The restoration of love in Christ, in whom the wounds of the fall begin to be undone, Romans 8:19 to 21.

typology.type: The bride's invitation, come, my beloved
typology.fulfillment: The church's answering longing for her Lord, the Spirit and the bride say, come, Revelation 22:17.

study.christ: The refrain of this movement is astonishing: his desire is for me. It is one thing to know that we should desire God; it is another to hear that His desire is for His people. The love of Christ for His church is not cool duty but glad longing; He set His heart upon her, gave Himself for her, and waits to bring her home. And the bride's free invitation, come, my beloved, is the church's answering cry, the very words with which Scripture ends, the Spirit and the bride say, come, come, Lord Jesus. The longing strained by the fall is here healed into the joy of covenant love that points to Him.
study.god: God is not grudging toward His people; His desire is for them. He heals what sin strained, turning grasping and domination into mutual delight, and He gladly receives the love His people freely offer. He made the intimacy of husband and wife and calls it good, and He is not embarrassed by joy. As a bridegroom rejoices over his bride, so God rejoices over His own.

crossReferences.note: The word desire first names the strained longing between the sexes after the fall; here it is turned to joy, his desire is for me.
The one flesh union is a profound mystery that signifies Christ and the church, the frame for reading the lovers' mutual belonging.
The Spirit and the bride say, come, echoing the bride's invitation, come, my beloved.
As a bridegroom rejoices over the bride, so will your God rejoice over you, the delight the Song pictures.

study.oneStory: The bridegroom's praise is met by the bride's glad answer: she belongs to him, his desire is for her, and she freely invites him to the vineyards to give him her love. Desire once strained by the fall is healed into joy, and the mutual longing of the lovers points beyond every marriage to the love between Christ and His church, who together say, come.

quiz.q: How does the bridegroom sum up his praise of the bride in 7:6?

options.text: How fair and pleasant you are, O love, with your delights.
options.note: He addresses the bride herself as love, delighting in her whole person.

options.text: How plain and forgettable you are.
options.note: He praises her as fair and pleasant, the opposite of forgettable.

options.text: How useful you are to me.
options.note: She is indeed precious to him, but his words are delight and affection in her whole person, not a matter of mere usefulness.

options.text: How much you must still improve.
options.note: He does lavish attention on her, but he delights in her as she is, calling her fair and pleasant, with nothing she must still improve.

quiz.q: What refrain does the bride speak in 7:10?

options.text: I belong to my beloved, and his desire is for me.
options.note: The second great refrain of belonging, now stressing his desire for her.

options.text: I hope my beloved might one day want me.
options.note: She does speak of belonging and his desire, but as a settled present reality, his desire is for me now, not merely a hope that he might one day want her.

options.text: My beloved desires many others.
options.note: His desire is for her alone, the language of exclusive covenant love.

options.text: I belong to myself alone.
options.note: She does have a real dignity of her own in the Song, but this refrain is mutual belonging, I am his and his desire is for me, not belonging to herself alone.

quiz.q: Why is the word desire in 7:10 so striking?

options.text: It is the word from Genesis 3:16 for strained longing after the fall, here healed into joy.
options.note: The same rare word, once a mark of the fall's strain, is turned to mutual delight.

options.text: It means the beloved is forced against his will.
options.note: It speaks of glad longing freely given, not compulsion.

options.text: It has no background elsewhere in Scripture.
options.note: It is indeed a rare and notable word, but it does have pointed echoes elsewhere, in Genesis 3 and 4, not no background at all.

options.text: It proves desire is always sinful.
options.note: It does carry the background of the fall from Genesis 3:16, but here that strained desire is healed into good, redeemed longing within covenant love, not proof that desire is always sinful.

quiz.q: What does the bride do after the refrain of belonging?

options.text: She invites her beloved out to the vineyards, there I will give you my love.
options.note: She takes the lead with a glad, free invitation.

options.text: She sends her beloved away.
options.note: She does call him to go out to the vineyards, but to go together, come my beloved, not to send him away.

options.text: She falls silent and withdraws.
options.note: She speaks up gladly and invites him.

options.text: She waits passively for him to act.
options.note: Her invitation does await his coming with her, but she is not passive; she takes the lead and initiates it.

quiz.q: How does his desire is for me point to the gospel?

options.text: It pictures Christ's glad longing for His church, who answers, come, my beloved.
options.note: Christ's love for His people is glad desire, and the church answers with longing, come, Lord Jesus.

options.text: It teaches that God only tolerates His people.
options.note: God does patiently bear with His people, but the refrain is desire and delight, far more than mere toleration.

options.text: It shows that God keeps His distance from His people.
options.note: The point is nearness and longing, his desire is for me.

options.text: It has nothing to do with Christ and the church.
options.note: It truly speaks of a human beloved's desire, yet the church has long heard here the mutual love of Christ and His people, Revelation 22:17, so it does point to the gospel.

questions: The bride hears, his desire is for me. Have you let yourself believe that Christ's love for you is glad longing and not cool duty, and how would that change today?
The word desire, strained by the fall, is healed here into joy. Where do you long to see God heal a strained or broken relationship into delight?
The bride takes the lead with a warm invitation, come, my beloved. Where might God be calling you to seek Him gladly rather than waiting passively?
She had treasured up delicacies, new and old, for her beloved. What have you stored up, in devotion or affection, to offer the Lord or those you love?
The Song presents mutual desire within covenant as good and joyful. How does that shape the way you think and speak about marriage, the body, and longing?

translationNotes.note: The Hebrew rendered mouth is literally palate; the versions differ on the last line, the Hebrew reading gliding gently over lips as we sleep.
Desire, teshuqah, is a rare word, used of the woman toward her husband in Genesis 3:16 and of sin in Genesis 4:7; here it is turned to joy, his desire is for me.
The source em dash before there I will give you my love was rendered as a clean break with a comma; no dashes remain.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Song of Solomon 7:1-13, chapter 7. Verse text assembled by concatenating segment text values in order; the embedded speaker label The Bride within verse 9 was treated as a heading and excluded, and footnote markers were ignored. The source em dash in verse 12 (in bloom) was converted to a comma; no em or en dashes remain.
